statusLine: Americas energy markets under pressure — dual Hormuz/Red Sea closures compounding Atlantic Basin demand for US, Brazil, and Guyana crude
fullAnalysis:
The simultaneous closure of the Strait of Hormuz (18 April 2026) and ongoing Houthi disruption of Bab-el-Mandeb creates an unprecedented compound supply shock for global oil markets. With both primary Gulf export corridors disrupted, international buyers are increasingly pivoting to Atlantic Basin producers — a shift that directly benefits US shale exporters, Brazil's Petrobras pre-salt operations, and Guyana's fast-growing Stabroek block.

US commercial crude stocks and production data from EIA will be critical indicators over the coming weeks. The US strategic petroleum reserve (SPR) stands at approximately 370 million barrels following partial restoration from the 2022 emergency drawdown, providing meaningful buffer capacity. The Biden administration-era sales reduced SPR to multi-decade lows, but subsequent repurchases have restored partial cover.

WTI crude has rallied on the Hormuz closure news, narrowing its traditional discount to Brent as Gulf sour crude becomes harder to source. US Gulf Coast refineries optimised for light sweet crude may benefit from this dislocation — they can run domestic WTI feedstock while competitors face sour crude shortages. However, refinery complexity varies significantly, and some Gulf Coast facilities are specifically configured for heavy Venezuelan or Mexican Maya crude.

Looking across the hemisphere, Canada's oil sands output remains robust but Western Canadian Select continues to trade at a discount reflecting pipeline export constraints. The Trans Mountain Expansion (TMX) pipeline, which opened in 2024, has improved Pacific access for Canadian crude but volumes remain below design capacity.
keyPoints: Dual Hormuz + Red Sea closures creating unprecedented global supply shock — benefits Americas Atlantic Basin producers
US shale exporters, Brazil Petrobras, and Guyana Stabroek positioned as swing suppliers to Asian and European buyers
WTI-Brent spread narrowing as Gulf sour crude scarcity drives international buyers toward light sweet alternatives
US SPR at ~370MB following post-2022 partial restoration — meaningful buffer but below pre-2022 levels
Panama Canal water levels returning to normal after 2023-24 drought restrictions — Americas Pacific routes reopening
